title: Pass by Reference 📬
description: How to edit remote variables directly.

slides.title: The Clone Problem 🐑
slides.content:
Normally, when you pass a variable into a function in C, it creates a **copy** (a clone) of the value.

If the function changes the copy, the original variable back in `main()` is completely unaffected! This is called **Pass by Value**.

slides.title: Pass by Value Fails
slides.content: Look closely: `tryToCheat()` changes its cloned copy of `health`, but the real `health` in `main()` stays at 100.

quizData.question: By default, passing an integer to a function in C gives the function direct access to edit the original variable.
quizData.explanation: No! By default, C strictly hands functions a photocopy of the data. The original is safe from modification.

slides.title: The Solution: Pass by Reference 📬
slides.content:
To let a function edit the original variable, you don't pass the photocopy. You pass the **Memory Address** (using `&`).

The function parameters receive a **Pointer** (`*`), which allows it to "Dereference" the address and remotely edit the original box!

slides.title: Working Code
slides.content: Notice how we pass `&health` and the function accepts `int *hpPtr`.

quizData.question: If changing a pointer's target is so dangerous, why is Pass by Reference useful?

options.text: It lets a function quickly modify massive amounts of data without wasting CPU time making millions of photocopies.
It makes the code look more professional.
It is exactly as slow as making photocopies but safer.

quizData.explanation: Passing a heavy 1GB file by value creates a 1GB copy, freezing the computer. Passing a pointer just hands over an 8-byte treasure map pointing to the 1GB file.
